source_topic: How quantum computers use superposition to compute differently

scenes.narration: Google's quantum chip solved a problem in two hours. The world's fastest supercomputer would need three-point-two years. Here's why.
A classical bit is heads or tails, never both. A qubit exists in both states simultaneously, until you measure it. That's superposition.

props.fallbackText: Classical bits are 0 OR 1. Qubits are 0 AND 1 simultaneously — until measured.

scenes.narration: IBM's Quantum Composer: the H gate creates superposition. CNOT entangles two qubits. From here, the circuit explores every computational path at once.

props.fallbackText: IBM Quantum Composer circuit: H gate (superposition) + CNOT gate (entanglement) + measurement.

scenes.narration: The key is interference. Quantum circuits are designed so wrong computational paths cancel each other out. Only the correct answer survives to be measured.
Google's Willow chip ran a physics simulation thirteen thousand times faster than the world's fastest supercomputer. Verified in twenty twenty-five, not theoretical.

props.fallbackText: Google Willow quantum chip: 13,000× faster than Frontier supercomputer. Verified 2025.

scenes.narration: Add one qubit, double your states. At three hundred qubits, you're simultaneously holding more states than atoms in the observable universe.

props.label: simultaneous states in a 300-qubit system — more than atoms in the universe

scenes.narration: Quantum computers won't replace your laptop. They target a specific problem class — and that class is growing. Follow for one concept a day.

caption:
Your computer checks one answer at a time. A quantum chip checked all of them — and still finished in 2 hours.

Superposition lets qubits be in every state simultaneously. But the real trick is interference — wrong answers cancel each other out, the right one amplifies and survives.

300 qubits holds more states than atoms in the observable universe.

Save this — quantum just went mainstream.
